coredata.dc:title: Multicolor fluorescent/room temperature phosphorescent carbon dot composites for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hongmei
Sun, Lili
Guo, Xiangjun
Xu, Jiyao
Zhao, Xihui
Xia, Yanzhi
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have a place in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications due to features such as long-life luminescence. In this work, three different colors (blue, cyan and green) carbon dots, named B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using glutathione, m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials. B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs generate more n-π* transitions due to the doping of N atoms, which promotes inter-system crossing (ISC) and thus effectively populates triplet excitons. In addition, the corresponding fluorescent/phosphorescent solid powders were obtained by dispersing B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs in sodium alginate (SA). After turning off the UV lamp, several seconds of RTP were able to be observed with phosphorescence lifetimes of 165.8, 284.5, and 186.1 ms, respectively. Moreover, RTP is easily quenched by atmospheric oxygen and water molecules, so green RTP can only be observed in a dry condition by printing C-CDs@SA gels on filter paper. Based on this feature, the prepared CDs can be effectively used for message encryption and advanced anti-counterfeiting. As well as, C-CDs@SA powder also has important applications in fingerprint detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Carbon dots
Nitrogen doping
Anti-counterfeiting
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and EcoTextiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of B-CDs (A), C-CDs (B), and G-CDs (C). Insets: the corresponding particle size distribution histograms. HR-TEM lattice fringe image of B-CDs (D), C-CDs (E), and G-CDs (F).
XPS full spectrum (A) and high-resolution spectra of C1s (B), O1s (C), and N1s (D) of the B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs.

Excitation, emission and UV − vis absorption spectra of B-CDs (A), C-CDs (B), and G-CDs (C). Insets: the corresponding photographs under daylight (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). FL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of B-CDs (D), C-CDs (E), and G-CDs (F). The CIE coordinates of B-CDs (G), C-CDs (H), and G-CDs (I).

Phosphorescence spectra of B-CDs@SA (A), C-CDs@SA (B), and G-CDs@SA (C) with different excitation wavelengths. (D) Digital photographs of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA under sun light, 365 nm light irradiation, and those taken from 0.1 to 3.0 s after turning off the 365 nm light. (E) Phosphorescence decay curves of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA at room temperature after 370 nm, 420 nm, and 400 nm light irradiation, respectively.

(A) Optical photographs of C-CDs@SA under sunlight and before and after UV excitation. (B) Fluorescence/phosphorescence photographs and schematic diagrams of C-CDs@SA in dry and wet states. (C) Fingerprint images of C-CDs@SA powder on stainless steel items under sunlight and 365 nm UV excitation.

(A) Schematic of the preparation procedure of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs. (B) Photos of B-CDs (left), C-CDs (middle), and G-CDs (right) were exposed to sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light. (C) Possible phosphorescence emission processes for the B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs. Abs. absorbance, Fluo. fluorescence, Phos. phosphorescence, Non-rad. Non-radiation.

head.ce:title: Multicolor fluorescent/room temperature phosphorescent carbon dot composites for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles
Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have a place in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications due to features such as long-life luminescence. In this work, three different colors (blue, cyan and green) carbon dots, named B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using glutathione, m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials. B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs generate more n-π* transitions due to the doping of N atoms, which promotes inter-system crossing (ISC) and thus effectively populates triplet excitons. In addition, the corresponding fluorescent/phosphorescent solid powders were obtained by dispersing B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs in sodium alginate (SA). After turning off the UV lamp, several seconds of RTP were able to be observed with phosphorescence lifetimes of 165.8, 284.5, and 186.1 ms, respectively. Moreover, RTP is easily quenched by atmospheric oxygen and water molecules, so green RTP can only be observed in a dry condition by printing C-CDs@SA gels on filter paper. Based on this feature, the prepared CDs can be effectively used for message encryption and advanced anti-counterfeiting. As well as, C-CDs@SA powder also has important applications in fingerprint detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials are afterglow materials that utilize light to store energy. After the excitation light source is removed, the stored energy is slowly released as photons within the material, resulting in a self-sustained luminescence of a few seconds or hours[1–4]. RTP materials are uniquely characterized by long-lived triplet-state excited states, and the ability to effectively eliminate self-absorption and background fluorescence[5,6]. Therefore, it has more attractive application prospects than fluorescent materials in the fields of information encryption, advanced anti-counterfeiting, organic light-emitting diodes, bio-imaging, etc.[7–11]. Among them, people have been paying high attention to information encryption and anti-counterfeiting issues because they are of great significance to the political and economic security of the country as well as the safety of people's lives and properties. The new anti-counterfeiting technology developed by using RTP has the advantages of advanced encryption that is difficult to imitate, simple operation, and low requirements for instruments, which exhibits huge advantages in anti-counterfeiting[12].

However, influenced by the external environment (atmospheric oxygen and water molecules, etc.), the triplet-state excitons of RTP materials are susceptible to deactivation by nonradiative transitions, resulting in short (µs-ms) and the rapid decay of the phosphorescent emission of RTP materials, which greatly limits the application of RTP in real-life applications[13]. Therefore, the preparation of long afterglow RTP materials remains a challenge. At present, most RTP materials mainly focus on organometallic complexes and metal-free pure organic systems with a wide range of emission wavelengths and easy molecular modifications[14–17]. However, organic systems usually have disadvantages such as high toxicity, high cost, complicated preparation, or only in crystalline/composite form[18]. Therefore, it is of considerable scientific significance to develop an environmentally friendly, low-cost, and easily synthesized long-life RTP material.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of luminescent nanomaterial, have become a better choice in building RTP systems due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, easy synthesis, and unique optical properties[19–22]. Special surface groups (e.g. C═N, C═O) on CDs interact with CDs to stabilize the triplet state and facilitate the inter-system crossing (ISC) from the excited state (S1) to the triplet state (Tn)[23]. However, not all CDs have significant RTP effect, which requires some processing of CDs. From previous studies, it is known that doping heteroatoms such as N, P, and F in CDs can produce more n → π* transitions, promote ISC, and further generate triplet excitons[9,24–26], or disperse CDs in polyvinyl alcohol[18], melamine[27], zeolite[28], boron oxide[29], and other substrates to form hydrogen/covalent bonds to inhibit nonradiative transitions, can promote and optimize the phosphorescence of CDs. Recently, Yang's group has developed RTP materials with green excitation and wide emission wavelengths by doping CDs with N, and P elements and embedding CDs in a melamine matrix[30]. These treatments of CDs promote ISC, populate triplet excitons efficiently, and their rigid environment limits molecular motion and nonradiative transitions as a means to achieve long-life RTPs. In addition, because of their well-soluble in water and ultra-small size, CDs can be easily coated on paper or other substrates[14]. They are valuable in practical anti-counterfeiting applications.

Additionally, the properties of CDs can be improved by doping heteroatoms to adjust the microstructure of the carbon core and surface[31]. In this work, blue, cyan, and green fluorescent carbon dots, named B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, respectively, were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal carbonization method using glutathione, m-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials. Significantly, glutathione, a tripeptide containing γ-amide bonds and sulfhydryl groups, can dope heteroatoms into CDs and modify the surface of CDs with functional groups during carbonization. m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine have rigid conjugated carbon skeleton structures based on amino-substituted benzene rings, which are good choices for tuning carbon core structures. As a direct benefit, the as-prepared CDs are nitrogen-doped, highly surface-modified, and have a certain degree of crystallinity. More importantly, B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs were dispersed in a sodium alginate matrix to further obtain three solid-state fluorescence/RTP carbon dots composites with different colors and phosphorescent lifetimes. The obtained composites (named B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA) not only displayed blue, cyan, and green fluorescence (FL) under a 365 nm UV lamp, but also displayed blue, green, and yellow RTP after removing the UV source, respectively. It is worth mentioning that since RTP is extremely susceptible to quenching by water molecules in the air, C-CDs@SA gels coated on filter paper can only produce RTP in a dry condition, while RTP cannot be observed in a humid condition, which is a two-way iterative process. Based on this feature, C-CDs@SA' FL and RTP can be changed at will in secure mode, making them more reliable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. In addition, C-CDs@SA powder was used to detect fingerprints remaining on objects, and fluorescent images with clearly discernible fingerprint details were obtained under UV light, which indicates that it is a good tool for detecting fingerprints.

Glutathione was purchased by Love Biological Technology Co., ltd. (shanghai, China). M−phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine, carrageenan (κ-Carr), chitosan (CTS), gelatin (GEL) and algal fiber (ALG) were purchased by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Sodium alginate (SA) was purchased from Tianjin Balance Biotechnology Co., ltd. Agar was purchased from Beijing Land Bridge Technology Co., ltd. Unless otherwise noted, all reagents were used without purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were observed on a JEM-1200EX transmission microscope (JOEL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained on a NICOLET 5700 spectrometer with KBr spheres (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Analysis Instrument Co., ltd. Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectra measurements were performed on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence decay curve was performed with Edinburgh FLS980. Phosphorescence spectrograms (the range of detection wavelengths of B-CDs, C-CDS, and G-CDs were 330–370 nm, 360–440 nm, and 370–430 nm, respectively), phosphorescence lifetimes (the excitation wavelengths of B-CDs, C-CDS, and G-CDs were 370 nm, 420 nm, and 400 nm, respectively), and phosphorescence quantum yields (the excitation wavelengths of B-CDs, C-CDS, and G-CDs were 350 nm, 410 nm, and 400 nm, respectively) were performed on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a microsecond flash (μF) and a time-selected pass.

Blue carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using glutathione as the raw material. Specifically, 0.5 g of glutathione and 10 mL of deionized water were homogeneously mixed, and the reaction solution was transferred to a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in a muffle furnace at 220 °C for 18 h. After the reaction, it was naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain the brown CDs solution, and then centrifuged at 1000 rpm/min for 15 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO═1000, American Thermo Fisher) in ultrapure water for 18 h. The clear and transparent light yellow carbon dot solution was finally obtained and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experimental applications. Cyan fluorescent carbon dots (C-CDs) and green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) were prepared in the same way as above except that 0.1 g of m-phenylenediamine and 0.1 g of p-phenylenediamine were added as nitrogen source, respectively, and hydrothermally reacted with 0.5 g of glutathione to obtain clear yellow and brown solutions. Then stored at 4 °C for further application.

Weighed 1.0 g of SA in three beakers, and added 1 mL 3.0 mg/mL of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, respectively. Stirred well and placed in an oven at 80 °C for 10 h. After drying, the fluorescent/phosphorescent solid powders based on SA were obtained by grinding and named B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, particle size, and dispersion of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs were characterized with TEM. The morphologies of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs were all quasi-spherical, B-CDs and C-CDs showed good mono-dispersity, while the dispersed morphology of G-CDs was not completely monodispersed (Fig. 1 A-C), probably due to the stronger mutual attraction in G-CDs[32]. As seen in the particle size distribution histograms (Fig. 1A-C, inset), the size distributions of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs are narrow, with the average grain size of B-CDs being about 2.8 nm, while the average grain sizes of C-CDs and G-CDs increase to 6.5 nm and 7.5 nm, respectively. HR-TEM images (Fig. 1D-F) show good resolution for B -CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs with lattice fringe spacing of 0.23 nm, 0.22 nm, and 0.24 nm, respectively, which corresponds to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphene, indicating that the carbon dots have satisfactory crystallinity[33]. Subsequently, the surface functional groups and chemical structures of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs were explored by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Figure S1, the sharp absorption band at 3432 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibrations of O H and N H[34], while the absorption peaks at 2929–2850 cm−1 belong to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of C H[31]. It is confirmed by the peak at 1600 cm−1 for the aromatic C═C bond, and the absorption bands at 1428, 1130, and 784 cm−1 correspond to C N, C O C stretching vibrations and C H out-of-plane bending vibrations[34–36], where the FT-IR spectra of C-CDs and G-CDs are all enhanced in intensity at 1428 cm−1 compared to those of B-CDs, which implies an increase in nitrogen content. FT-IR results confirmed that CDs were functionalized by hydroxyl (−OH), carboxyl (−COOH), amino (−NH2) groups, contributing to the excellent water solubility and stability of CDs[37,38].

In addition, to further explore the structure and chemical composition of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, XPS tests were performed. The XPS full-scan spectra of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs have three characteristic peaks at 285 eV, 399 eV, and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. As can be observed in Fig. 2 A, the main difference between these three CDs is the nitrogen content, which is 0.62 %, 6.42 %, and 3.86 % in that order. Among them, the electron donor conjugation effect of the amino group on the benzene ring makes p-phenylenediamine more easily oxidized than m-phenylenediamine, which may in turn results in different N content doped into C-CDs and G-CDs during carbonization. And the content of doped N atoms may be the main reason for the differences in the luminescence properties of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs. The C1s XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 2B) confirm the presence of C═C/C C (284.76 eV), C O/C N (285.84 eV), and O═C O/N (288.33 eV) in the B-CDs, the binding energies at 284.32 eV, 284.90 eV, 285.79 eV, and 288.36 eV in the C1s spectra of C-CDs represent C C, C═C, C O/C N, and O═C O/N, respectively. And similarly, the C1s spectra of G-CDs have these chemical bonds at 284.18 eV, 284.76 eV, 285.53 eV, and 288.32 eV, respectively[39]. Both peaks in the high-resolution spectra of the O1s of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs (Fig. 2C) indicate the presence of − COOH/O═C C (∼531.3 eV) and C − OH/C O C (∼532.0 eV)[40]. The N1s XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 2D) demonstrated the presence of N atoms mainly in the form of porphyrin C N C (∼399 eV) and amino N (∼401 eV) [31,39,41]. N atoms can introduce new surface states (N states). The electrons trapped by the N state can realize high-yield radiation recombination and suppress non-radiation recombination. In addition, the amino group is an electron donor, which can improve the conjugation degree of CDs, generate higher electron density, and enhance the binding force[6,42].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs in an aqueous solution were measured to investigate their optical properties. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3 A-C, black lines) showed that B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs have two UV absorption peaks at 255 nm and 340 nm, which are attributed to π → π * and n → π * electron transitions in the carbon core, respectively. In addition, compared with B-CDs, C-CDS and G-CDs have a broader UV absorption tail in the visible range of 400–500 nm, which is mainly attributed to the C═N-related surface groups. [43–45]. The aqueous solution of B-CDs exhibited pale yellow color under sunlight and blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 385 nm and 444 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). And the position of the maximum emission peak was red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 3D), showing a significant excitation dependence, which is not only related to quantum size effects but also to the complex surface states and edge states. The surface functional groups related to C═O and C═N induce trap states at different energy levels, as well as the low passivation by − NH2 /−OH on the surface of CDs, account for excitation-dependent emission[46,47]. The maximum value of the CIE coordinates of the fluorescence spectra was (0.17, 0.19), further supporting that the B-CDs have a significant blue emission (Fig. 3G). According to Fig. 3B, the yellow transparent solution of C-CDs revealed cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, and the optimal emission wavelength of C-CDs was 485 nm under 410 nm excitation. The fluorescence emission spectrum of C-CDs (Fig. 3E) exhibited a slight excitation dependence with an optimal value of (0.15, 0.28) for the CIE coordinates (Fig. 3H), further indicating the cyan fluorescence emission. It can be observed from Fig. 3C that the maximum emission wavelength of G-CDs is 513 nm under excitation at 370 nm, the aqueous solution of G-CDs is brownish yellow under daylight and green fluorescence under UV light. Unlike the single-peak fluorescence emission of B-CDs and C-CDs, G-CDs produce two emission peaks at 440 and 510 nm under excitation at 300–370 nm, while the excitation wavelength is in the range of 380–430 nm with only one emission peak at 510 nm (Fig. 3F), which may be due to the fact that the short-wavelength emission originates from the quantum confinement effect of the carbon core and the long-wavelength emission originates from the energy trap of the surface states. As the excitation wavelength increases, the excitation energy is not sufficient to drive the transition of the sp2 domain π → π * of the carbon core, making the emission change from a double peak to a single[42,48,49]. The optimal coordinate of the CIE coordinate plot (Fig. 3I) was (0.25, 0.42), and the green fluorescence emission could be clearly observed, which was consistent with the results reflected by the fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs. Furthermore, the analysis of the fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs (Figure S2) showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs were 3.4 ns, 7.1 ns, and 4.4 ns, respectively.

It is well known that CDs always suffer from serious self-quenching in the solid state and short-term or even no RTP due to π–π stacking interactions, aggregation, or excessive nonradiative energy transfer[50]. Dispersing CDs in a polymer or solid matrix to realize solid-state fluorescence/RTP in CDs is a common strategy. Therefore, B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs were dispersed in the SA matrix to obtain the corresponding solid-state CDs@SA composites (B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA). SA has no fluorescence and phosphorescence, but the obtained B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA not only displayed solid-state fluorescence but also possessed RTP properties. SA is a natural polysaccharide extracted from seaweed, which has the advantages of easy availability of raw materials, low price, and no pollution to the environment. Moreover, during this process, SA not only increases the distance between CDs as a dispersant and further suppresses the solid-state quenching of CDs, but also endows CDs with film-forming characteristics, which are beneficial in many applications. More importantly, the hydroxyl group on the SA molecular chain can form hydrogen bonds with the functional groups on the surface of CDs and provide a rigid environment to protect the phosphorescence from being quenched. To further investigate the phosphorescence properties, phosphorescence spectra of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA were measured. It is evident from Fig. 4 A-C that the wavelength corresponding to the maximum emission peak of B-CDs@SA is almost unchanged, while the phosphorescence spectra of both C-CDs@SA and G-CDs@SA exhibit a slight red shift with excitation dependence. The maximum phosphorescence emission wavelength of B-CDs@SA at 350 nm excitation is 423 nm, the position of the most intense emission peak of C-CDs@SA appears at about 490 nm, and the wavelength corresponding to the maximum emission peak of G-CDs@SA is about 525 nm. These locations where the maximum emission wavelengths appear to confirm that B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA have blue, green, and yellow phosphorescence colors, respectively. In Fig. 4D, it can be visually observed that the powders of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA have light yellow, yellow and brown colors under daylight, also could demonstrate blue, cyan, and green fluorescence under the excitation of UV light at 365 nm, respectively. When turned off the UV light, the powders of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA continued to glow in blue, green, and yellow phosphorescent for several seconds until they disappeared. The phosphorescence phenomenon of C-CDs@SA can be watched in full from the video (Video S1), showing about 3 s of green room temperature phosphorescence, which can be easily recognized with the naked eyes, which is a typical feature of afterglow materials. In addition, the phosphorescence decay curves of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA were measured (Fig. 4E). The solid powder of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA presented millisecond (ms)-based phosphorescence lifetimes of 165.8 ms, 284.5 ms, and 186.1 ms, and their phosphorescence quantum yields were measured to be 6.90 %, 21.62 %, and 7.34 %, respectively. Among them, C-CDs@SA had the best phosphorescence lifetime and phosphorescence quantum yield, which may be related to the fact that C-CDs possess the highest nitrogen content. It has been reported that N atoms have a significant effect on the phosphorescence of carbon dots that can generate higher electron density, leading to molecular orbital hybridization and thus promoting ISC production[6]. Later on, to clarify the effect of different substrates on phosphorescence, experiments were conducted with C-CDs which have a more significant phosphorescence effect. C-CDs were dispersed in SA, κ-Carr, Agar, and CTS, and then dried to obtain phosphorescent powder (Figure S3). The phosphorescence phenomenon was observed in the dark box, and the comparison showed that the phosphorescence effect of C-CDs dispersed in the SA and κ-Carr matrix was significant, and the green phosphorescence could be observed by the naked eyes for 3 s. While, the phosphorescence phenomenon was not apparent and was of short duration as C-CDs were dispersed in Agar and CTS. This may be due to the abundant negative charge functional groups on the surface of SA (–COO-) and κ-Carr (-OSO3 -), which have strong interaction with C-CDs.

After analyzing the above findings, the formation and origin of RTP of CD composites were proposed. As shown in Scheme 1 A, glutathione underwent self-polymerization and carbonization to finally produce B-CDs, then used glutathione as a precursor and m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine as nitrogen sources to synthesize C-CDs and G-CDs through carbonization. Subsequently, three CD composites were prepared using SA as a matrix showing different phosphorescent colors, mainly caused by the difference in the content of N atoms. The afterglow luminescence of CD composites is first attributed to C═O/C═N-related functional groups, which exhibit strong spin–orbit coupling and effectively generate triplet-state excitons[51,52]. And the doping of N elements can generate abundant surface states on CDs while promoting spin–orbit coupling[51,53]. More n-π* transitions are formed in C-CDs and G-CDs due to the doping of N atoms, and the modulation of the single-linear state-triplet energy splitting caused by the n-π* transitions contributes to the efficiency of ISC and the triplet state capture from S1 to T1 [54], thus increasing the number of triplets excited states of RTP. Therefore, the phosphorescence lifetimes of both C-CDs@SA and G-CDs@SA are enhanced compared with those of B-CDs@SA. Further, because RTP is easily quenched by water molecules and atmospheric oxygen, RTP can only be observed under dry conditions[55]. As shown in Scheme 1B, blue, cyan, and green fluorescence can be observed in the aqueous solutions of B-CDs, C-CDs, G-CDs, and powders of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, G-CDs@SA under 365 nm UV light. However, blue, green, and yellow phosphorescence was only observed in the dried powders of B-CDs@SA, C-CDs@SA, and G-CDs@SA. This is because B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs have abundant functional groups (−OH, −COOH, and − NH2) on their surfaces, which allow them to form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups of SA and enhance the rigidity of the whole system, thus acting as a barrier to prevent the penetration of oxygen and moisture and protecting the phosphorescence from being quenched[33]. The possible emission process of phosphorescence is further analyzed by Scheme 1C, which shows that after absorbing UV energy, most of the energy returns from the excited state S1 to the ground state S0 in the form of photons, producing fluorescence. A small portion of the energy reaches the triple-excited state T1 from the single-excited state S1 through ISC, and a stable triple-state is formed under dry conditions because the presence of hydrogen bonding inhibits the non-radiative transition, resulting in the phosphorescence phenomenon[56]. In contrast, after water spraying, the rigid state of the system is destroyed under humid conditions, and the quenching of phosphorescence by atmospheric oxygen and water molecules cannot be prevented, and the energy is released rapidly due to the non-radiative transition, causing phosphorescence can not be produced[55]. These suggest that both sodium alginate and dry conditions play a crucial role in phosphorescent solid powders. Also, strong hydrogen bonding and the maintenance of a rigid state in the air are prerequisites for achieving RTP.

The existence of phosphorescence of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs composites are morphologically diverse and not limited to powders. The same experimental phenomena as those described above for fluorescent/phosphorescent solid powders were also observed for fluorescent/phosphorescent foams with high mechanical strength obtained by freeze-drying of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs dispersed in GEL (Figure S4). B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs could also be embeded in ALG to obtain the corresponding fluorescent/phosphorescent CDs@ALG (Figure S5). While, the pure ALG were white, and did not possess fluorescence and phosphorescence. Significantly, the phosphorescence phenomenon of CDs@ALG was weakened relative to the CDs@SA powder, and the phosphorescence of the C-CDs@ALG was probably maintained for only 1 s as seen in the video (Video S2). The phosphor phenomenon could not even be appreciated obviously by the naked eyes in G-CDs-doped ALG, whereas only white fluorescence due to the overlapping of blue and yellow fluorescence was visible under UV light irradiation. These phenomena indicate that the dispersion of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs in ALG is relatively poor as well as the phosphorescence effect is cut down. However, the diverse phosphorescent presence morphology broadens their application range and has vital research significance.

The excellent RTP performance of B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs composites makes them promising for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. C-CDs were used as a model dispersed in the SA and dried at 70 °C for 6 h. C-CDs-doped SA films were obtained, which emitted cyan fluorescence under UV, and the C-CDs@SA films continued to produce green phosphorescence when the UV light source was cut off (Figure S6). Based on this property of C-CDs@SA film, the anti-counterfeiting application of C-CDs in screen printing was extended in this work. A custom stencil with Qingdao University lettering was used for printing, and the SA solution with C-CDs added was printed on the filter paper. After printing and drying, the pattern is not visible under sunlight, and a clear pattern can be observed under 365 nm UV excitation. After turning off the UV lamp, the pattern is still visible within 1 s, and the pattern details can still be distinguished (Fig. 5 A). Therefore, we can use this technology to print anti-counterfeiting patterns on materials such as books, invoices, and banknotes to achieve advanced anti-counterfeiting applications. More importantly, phosphorescence is easily quenched by water molecules in the air, so the RTP phenomenon cannot be observed in a wet environment. As shown in the phosphorescence photograph and schematic in Fig. 5B, the cyan fluorescence is always observed in both dry and wet environments, while the phosphorescence of the C-CDs@SA film can only be produced when dry and quenched when wet. However, it is a repeatable process, and the green RTP will appear again after the wet filter paper has been dried again. This certainly adds another layer of security for advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. Finally, we investigated the application of C-CDs@SA powder in fingerprint imaging, as shown in Fig. 5C. Fingerprint traces left on stainless steel items were detected with C-CDs@SA powder with fainter imaging under daylight while showing higher contrast in development under UV excitation. Under UV light, the striation details of the fingerprints, such as termination, crossover, core, scar, bifurcation, and island are clearly displayed with higher resolution, helping us to easily obtain level 2 and level 3 information about the fingerprints and providing a good method for analysis to determine the owner of the fingerprints. In addition, the fingerprints were printed on different objects such as beakers, discs, paper, and ceramic cups (Figure S7) and coated with C-CDs@SA powder, which also showed cyan fluorescence and high clarity under UV excitation. The successful application on different materials shows that C-CDs@SA powder has an excellent performance in fingerprint detection.

In summary, three fluorescent carbon dots with different colors, namely B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, were synthesized using glutathione, m-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine as the raw materials by hydrothermal method. Furthermore, the corresponding fluorescent/RTP CDs composites were obtained by dispersing B-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs in SA matrix, which not only showed blue, cyan, and green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, respectively, but also continued to emit light after removing the UV source, producing RTP with phosphorescence lifetimes of 165.8 ms, 284.5 ms, and 186.1 ms, respectively. The production of phosphorescence is related to the following two factors: (i) The doping of N atoms introduces more n-π* transitions for C-CDs and G-CDs, which is beneficial to promote ISC and further excite triplet state excitons to form RTPs with low energy gaps; (ii) the abundant − OH on the surface of SA forms hydrogen bonds with C═O and C═N/C N groups in the CDs, limiting intramolecular motion and inhibiting nonradiative transitions, protecting phosphorescence from quenching by atmospheric oxygen and water molecules. Finally, the C-CDs@SA gels were printed on filter paper by screen printing technique and dried, and green RTP was observed for 1 s in a dry condition, while it was not observed in a humid condition because the phosphorescence was quenched by water molecules, but it reappeared after drying. This behavior of FL and RTP to shift randomly in secure mode makes the prepared CDs promising for applications in information encryption and advanced anti-counterfeiting. As well as, the fluorescence of C-CDs@SA powder has effective applications in fingerprint imaging, where the imaging of fingerprint patterns has high definition and high resolution under UV light, which is suitable for various materials and broadens the application range of CD.

Hongmei Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lili Sun: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiangjun Guo: Formal analysis. Jiyao Xu: Formal analysis. Xihui Zhao: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yanzhi Xia: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and EcoTextiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.155945.
